Made with MyST

7. The general linear model

MATH 4210, Chapter 7

Leer en espanol

We return to the Dwaine Studios cities of 6. Matrix algebra for regression. The company runs a chain of portrait studios in mid-sized cities, the kind of place families visit for graduation and holiday photos, and it wants a rule for guessing how much a new studio will sell before it signs a lease. Two facts about a city are easy to look up in advance: the number of young people (population aged 16 or younger, in thousands) and the per-capita disposable income (in thousands of dollars). The question is whether those two numbers together predict annual studio sales well enough to guide the expansion.

In Chapter 6 you already assembled this fit by hand: you built the design matrix, formed X′X\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X}X′X and X′Y\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{Y}X′Y, inverted the small matrix, and solved for the coefficients b\mathbf{b}b. What that arithmetic could not tell you is why the recipe b=(X′X)−1X′Y\mathbf{b} = (\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{Y}b=(X′X)−1X′Y is the right one, or how far to trust the numbers it returns. This chapter proves why, and turns the fit into inference. Figure 1 shows the raw picture: sales climb with each predictor on its own, but neither plot alone tells you what the two predictors do together, and that “together” is the whole point of multiple regression.

Simple linear regression, from Chapters 2 and 3, handles one predictor. Real questions almost always come with several. We could try to rebuild the whole theory predictor by predictor, but that path gets ugly fast: two predictors, then three, each with its own normal equations and variance formulas. Instead we do what 6. Matrix algebra for regression set up. We write the model once, in matrix form, so that one predictor or ten predictors look identical on the page. Every result from Chapter 2, the least-squares estimates, their unbiasedness, their variances, the Gauss-Markov optimality, the n−2n-2n−2 divisor, reappears here in a single clean form that works for any number of predictors at once. This chapter is where regression stops being a collection of special cases and becomes one general method.

This is the most abstract chapter in the course, and worth a word on how to read it. The geometry in 7.2 The geometry of least squares is the idea to hold onto: once you picture fitting as dropping a perpendicular from the data onto the space of possible fits, every proof that follows reduces to one of two facts about the hat matrix. Hold that picture and the algebra becomes bookkeeping.

This lesson at a glance

  • What we are doing: Turning Chapter 6’s matrix arithmetic into a working multiple regression: deriving the least-squares estimator, its sampling distribution, and the Gauss-Markov theorem in matrix form.

  • Why we are doing it: Real questions come with several predictors, and rebuilding the theory one predictor at a time gets ugly; writing the model once in matrix form makes one predictor and ten look identical on the page.

  • Main objective: Derive b=(X′X)−1X′Y\mathbf{b} = (\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{Y}b=(X′X)−1X′Y by calculus and by projection, prove the hat-matrix and Gauss-Markov results, and recover every Chapter 2 formula as the p=2p = 2p=2 special case.

  • What changed from the last chapters: Chapter 6 supplied the matrix tools but stopped at the arithmetic; this chapter uses them to redo all of Chapter 2’s theory at once for any number of predictors, fitting the Dwaine model whose X′X\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X}X′X you already built (6. Matrix algebra for regression).

Learning objectives

By the end of this chapter you will be able to:

  • Write any linear regression as Y=Xβ+ε\mathbf{Y} = \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta} + \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}Y=Xβ+ε and identify the design matrix, coefficient vector, and error vector.

  • Derive the least-squares estimator b=(X′X)−1X′Y\mathbf{b} = (\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{Y}b=(X′X)−1X′Y two ways: by calculus and by orthogonal projection.

  • Interpret least squares geometrically as projecting Y\mathbf{Y}Y onto the column space of X\mathbf{X}X, with the residual perpendicular to that space.

  • Prove that the hat matrix H\mathbf{H}H is symmetric and idempotent with trace ppp, and use it to write fitted values and residuals.

  • Derive the mean and covariance of b\mathbf{b}b and state its sampling distribution under normal errors.

  • Prove that s2=MSE=SSE/(n−p)s^2 = \mathrm{MSE} = \mathrm{SSE}/(n-p)s2=MSE=SSE/(n−p) is unbiased for σ2\sigma^2σ2, using the trace of I−H\mathbf{I}-\mathbf{H}I−H.

  • State and prove the Gauss-Markov theorem in matrix form: b\mathbf{b}b is the best linear unbiased estimator.

  • Recover every simple-regression formula from Chapter 2 as the special case p=2p = 2p=2.

7.1 The model and least squares in matrix form¶

Intuition¶

In Chapter 2 a single predictor gave one slope and one intercept. Add a second predictor and you have two slopes plus an intercept; the mean response is now a tilted plane instead of a line, as Figure 2 shows for Dwaine. With more predictors it is a flat sheet in a space you cannot picture. Writing separate symbols for every coefficient and separate sums for every normal equation would bury the idea under bookkeeping. The fix is to stack the observations into vectors and matrices and let one line of matrix algebra carry all the bookkeeping for us.

The systematic part of each observation is a weighted sum of that city’s predictor values, the same weights (β0,β1,…\beta_0, \beta_1, \dotsβ0​,β1​,…) reused for every city. Stacking the cities vertically turns “the same weights applied to every row” into a single matrix-times-vector product. Figure 3 shows the four objects and their shapes.

Formula¶

The general linear model (Definition 7.1) collects all nnn observations into one matrix equation.

Definition 7.1: General linear model

The general linear model for nnn observations and ppp parameters is

Y=Xβ+ε,\mathbf{Y} = \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta} + \boldsymbol{\varepsilon},Y=Xβ+ε,

where Y\mathbf{Y}Y is the n×1n \times 1n×1 vector of responses, X\mathbf{X}X the n×pn \times pn×p design matrix, β\boldsymbol{\beta}β the p×1p \times 1p×1 vector of coefficients, and ε\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}ε the n×1n \times 1n×1 vector of random errors.

In words: each observation’s response equals a weighted sum of that observation’s predictor values plus a random error, and the same weights are reused for every row. The four objects are:

  • Y\mathbf{Y}Y is the n×1n \times 1n×1 vector of responses, one entry per observation.

  • X\mathbf{X}X is the n×pn \times pn×p design matrix (Definition 7.2). Its first column is all ones (for the intercept); each remaining column holds one predictor’s values. Row iii is city iii’s data.

  • β\boldsymbol{\beta}β is the p×1p \times 1p×1 vector of unknown parameters (β0,β1,…,βp−1)′(\beta_0, \beta_1, \dots, \beta_{p-1})'(β0​,β1​,…,βp−1​)′.

  • ε\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}ε is the n×1n \times 1n×1 vector of random errors.

  • ppp is the number of parameters, counting the intercept; with kkk predictors, p=k+1p = k + 1p=k+1. Here n=21n = 21n=21 and p=3p = 3p=3.

Definition 7.2: Design matrix

The design matrix X\mathbf{X}X is the n×pn \times pn×p matrix whose first column is all ones (carrying the intercept) and whose remaining columns each hold one predictor’s values, so row iii is observation iii’s predictor data.

The error assumptions of Chapter 2 become two compact statements about the whole vector at once:

E{ε}=0,Cov⁡{ε}=σ2I.E\{\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}\} = \mathbf{0}, \qquad \operatorname{Cov}\{\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}\} = \sigma^2 \mathbf{I} .E{ε}=0,Cov{ε}=σ2I.

In words: every error averages to zero, and the covariance matrix is σ2\sigma^2σ2 times the identity. That one symbol packs both assumptions at once: each error has the same variance σ2\sigma^2σ2 (down the diagonal), and distinct errors are uncorrelated (off the diagonal). Because X\mathbf{X}X and β\boldsymbol{\beta}β are fixed, E{Y}=XβE\{\mathbf{Y}\} = \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta}E{Y}=Xβ and Cov⁡{Y}=σ2I\operatorname{Cov}\{\mathbf{Y}\} = \sigma^2 \mathbf{I}Cov{Y}=σ2I as well. We assume throughout that X\mathbf{X}X has full column rank ppp: no predictor is a copy of another, or an exact linear combination of the others. That is exactly what makes X′X\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X}X′X invertible (6.4 The inverse and the normal equations).

To fit the model we reuse the least-squares idea from 2.2 Least squares from first principles: pick the coefficient vector b\mathbf{b}b that makes the fitted values closest to the data in total squared error. Writing a candidate vector as b\mathbf{b}b, the sum of squared errors is

Q(b)=∑i=1n(Yi−xi′b)2=(Y−Xb)′(Y−Xb),Q(\mathbf{b}) = \sum_{i=1}^n (Y_i - \mathbf{x}_i'\mathbf{b})^2 = (\mathbf{Y} - \mathbf{X}\mathbf{b})'(\mathbf{Y} - \mathbf{X}\mathbf{b}) ,Q(b)=i=1∑n​(Yi​−xi′​b)2=(Y−Xb)′(Y−Xb),

where xi′\mathbf{x}_i'xi′​ is row iii of X\mathbf{X}X. In words: QQQ is the squared length of the residual vector, the same quantity Chapter 2 minimized, now written as one dot product. The minimizer is the least-squares estimator (Theorem 7.3)

b=(X′X)−1X′Y.\mathbf{b} = (\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{Y} .b=(X′X)−1X′Y.

In words: gather the cross-products of the predictors (X′X\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X}X′X) and of the predictors with the response (X′Y\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{Y}X′Y), and multiply by the inverse of the first. We derive this next, then check it on Dwaine.

Derivation by calculus¶

Theorem 7.3: The least-squares estimator

Under the general linear model with X\mathbf{X}X of full column rank, the sum of squared errors Q(b)Q(\mathbf{b})Q(b) has a unique minimizer, the solution of the normal equations X′Xb=X′Y\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X}\mathbf{b} = \mathbf{X}'\mathbf{Y}X′Xb=X′Y:

b=(X′X)−1X′Y.\mathbf{b} = (\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{Y} .b=(X′X)−1X′Y.

Proof. Expand QQQ using the transpose rules of 6.2 Transpose and matrix multiplication. Since b′X′Y\mathbf{b}'\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{Y}b′X′Y is a scalar, it equals its own transpose Y′Xb\mathbf{Y}'\mathbf{X}\mathbf{b}Y′Xb, so the two middle terms combine:

Q(b)=Y′Y−2 b′X′Y+b′X′Xb.Q(\mathbf{b}) = \mathbf{Y}'\mathbf{Y} - 2\,\mathbf{b}'\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{Y} + \mathbf{b}'\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X}\mathbf{b} .Q(b)=Y′Y−2b′X′Y+b′X′Xb.

We minimize by taking the gradient with respect to b\mathbf{b}b and setting it to zero. The gradient is just the vector of partial derivatives, one per entry of b\mathbf{b}b, and setting it to zero finds the bottom of the bowl. Two vector-derivative rules do the work: for a constant vector a\mathbf{a}a, ∇b(a′b)=a\nabla_{\mathbf{b}}(\mathbf{a}'\mathbf{b}) = \mathbf{a}∇b​(a′b)=a; and for a symmetric matrix M\mathbf{M}M, ∇b(b′Mb)=2Mb\nabla_{\mathbf{b}}(\mathbf{b}'\mathbf{M}\mathbf{b}) = 2\mathbf{M}\mathbf{b}∇b​(b′Mb)=2Mb. These are the vector twins of the scalar rules ddb(ab)=a\frac{d}{db}(ab) = adbd​(ab)=a and ddb(mb2)=2mb\frac{d}{db}(mb^2) = 2mbdbd​(mb2)=2mb you already know. The matrix X′X\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X}X′X is symmetric, so

∇bQ=−2 X′Y+2 X′Xb.\nabla_{\mathbf{b}} Q = -2\,\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{Y} + 2\,\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X}\mathbf{b} .∇b​Q=−2X′Y+2X′Xb.

Setting the gradient to zero and dividing by 2 gives the normal equations (Definition 7.4)

X′Xb=X′Y.\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X}\mathbf{b} = \mathbf{X}'\mathbf{Y} .X′Xb=X′Y.

Because X\mathbf{X}X has full column rank, X′X\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X}X′X is invertible (6.4 The inverse and the normal equations), so we may multiply on the left by (X′X)−1(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}(X′X)−1:

b=(X′X)−1X′Y.\mathbf{b} = (\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{Y} .b=(X′X)−1X′Y.

This stationary point is the global minimum, not a saddle or maximum, because the matrix of second derivatives of QQQ is 2X′X2\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X}2X′X, which is positive definite; a quadratic with a positive-definite Hessian is strictly convex and has a single lowest point. ■\blacksquare■

Definition 7.4: Normal equations

The normal equations are the linear system

X′Xb=X′Y,\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X}\mathbf{b} = \mathbf{X}'\mathbf{Y},X′Xb=X′Y,

whose solution is the least-squares estimator. Equivalently, they state that the residual e=Y−Xb\mathbf{e} = \mathbf{Y} - \mathbf{X}\mathbf{b}e=Y−Xb is orthogonal to every column of X\mathbf{X}X.

These are the same normal equations as Chapter 2, now stated once for any number of predictors. In 2.2 Least squares from first principles the two scalar normal equations were ∑ei=0\sum e_i = 0∑ei​=0 and ∑Xiei=0\sum X_i e_i = 0∑Xi​ei​=0; the single matrix equation X′Xb=X′Y\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X}\mathbf{b} = \mathbf{X}'\mathbf{Y}X′Xb=X′Y contains both of those and one more for each extra predictor, as we will see in 7.7 Simple regression as a special case.

R¶

Chapter 6 built X′X\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X}X′X and X′Y\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{Y}X′Y for Dwaine by hand. Here we let the computer form them and solve.

dwaine <- read.csv("data/dwaine.csv")
head(dwaine, 3)
X <- cbind(1, dwaine$targtpop, dwaine$dispoinc)
Y <- dwaine$sales
dim(X)
  targtpop dispoinc sales
1     68.5     16.7 174.4
2     45.2     16.8 164.4
3     91.3     18.2 244.2
[1] 21  3
Example 7.1: Fitting Dwaine Studios by matrix algebra

Question. What are the least-squares coefficients for predicting studio sales from target population and disposable income?

Intuition. Form the cross-product matrix X′X\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X}X′X and the cross-product vector X′Y\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{Y}X′Y, then solve the normal equations by multiplying X′Y\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{Y}X′Y by (X′X)−1(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}(X′X)−1.

Formula. b=(X′X)−1X′Y\mathbf{b} = (\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{Y}b=(X′X)−1X′Y.

Computation.

XtX <- t(X) %*% X
XtY <- t(X) %*% Y
XtX
XtY
       [,1]     [,2]     [,3]
[1,]   21.0  1302.40   360.00
[2,] 1302.4 87707.94 22609.19
[3,]  360.0 22609.19  6190.26
          [,1]
[1,]   3820.00
[2,] 249643.35
[3,]  66072.75
XtX_inv <- solve(XtX)
b <- XtX_inv %*% XtY
b
          [,1]
[1,] -68.85707
[2,]   1.45456
[3,]   9.36550

The built-in lm fits the same model with a formula, and its coefficients agree to every printed digit.

coef(lm(sales ~ targtpop + dispoinc, data = dwaine))
(Intercept)    targtpop    dispoinc
  -68.85707     1.45456     9.36550

Now the same computation in Python, reading the same CSV.

import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import statsmodels.formula.api as smf

dwaine = pd.read_csv("data/dwaine.csv")
X = np.column_stack([np.ones(len(dwaine)), dwaine["targtpop"], dwaine["dispoinc"]])
Y = dwaine["sales"].to_numpy()
XtX = X.T @ X
XtY = X.T @ Y
b = np.linalg.inv(XtX) @ XtY
print(b)
print(smf.ols("sales ~ targtpop + dispoinc", data=dwaine).fit().params.values)
[-68.85707315   1.45455958   9.36550038]
[-68.85707315   1.45455958   9.36550038]

Interpretation. The fitted model is Y^=−68.86+1.4546 X1+9.3655 X2\hat{Y} = -68.86 + 1.4546\,X_1 + 9.3655\,X_2Y^=−68.86+1.4546X1​+9.3655X2​, with X1X_1X1​ the young-population count (thousands) and X2X_2X2​ disposable income (thousand dollars). Holding income fixed, each extra thousand young residents is associated with about $1{,}455 more in annual sales; holding population fixed, each extra thousand dollars of per-capita income is associated with about $9{,}366 more. The intercept -68.86 is the model’s sales at zero population and zero income, far outside the data, so it only anchors the plane. Chapter 8 develops the “holding the other predictor fixed” reading and its limits; here the point is that one matrix formula produced all three numbers at once.

Try it 7.1

The design matrix X\mathbf{X}X has n=21n = 21n=21 rows and p=3p = 3p=3 columns. Without running code, state the dimensions of X′X\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X}X′X, X′Y\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{Y}X′Y, b\mathbf{b}b, and the fitted vector Y^\hat{\mathbf{Y}}Y^, and say why XX′\mathbf{X}\mathbf{X}'XX′ would be the wrong matrix to invert.

Solution

X′X\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X}X′X is 3×33 \times 33×3 (p×pp \times pp×p), X′Y\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{Y}X′Y is 3×13 \times 13×1, b\mathbf{b}b is 3×13 \times 13×1, and Y^=Xb\hat{\mathbf{Y}} = \mathbf{X}\mathbf{b}Y^=Xb is 21×121 \times 121×1. The matrix XX′\mathbf{X}\mathbf{X}'XX′ is 21×2121 \times 2121×21 but has rank at most 3, so it is singular and cannot be inverted; the normal equations need the small full-rank p×pp \times pp×p matrix X′X\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X}X′X, not the large rank-deficient XX′\mathbf{X}\mathbf{X}'XX′.

7.2 The geometry of least squares¶

Intuition¶

The calculus derivation finds the answer but hides why it is the answer. There is a picture that makes least squares obvious, and it is worth carrying for the rest of the course. Think of the response Y\mathbf{Y}Y as a single arrow (a point) in nnn-dimensional space, one axis per observation. As the coefficient vector b\mathbf{b}b ranges over all possible values, the fitted vector Y^=Xb\hat{\mathbf{Y}} = \mathbf{X}\mathbf{b}Y^=Xb traces out a flat subspace: every linear combination of the columns of X\mathbf{X}X. This subspace is the column space of X\mathbf{X}X (Definition 7.5), and it holds every prediction the model can possibly make.

Definition 7.5: Column space

The column space of X\mathbf{X}X is the set of all vectors Xb\mathbf{X}\mathbf{b}Xb as b\mathbf{b}b ranges over every coefficient vector: a ppp-dimensional flat subspace of nnn-dimensional space that holds every fitted vector the model can produce.

The data point Y\mathbf{Y}Y almost never sits exactly in that subspace, because real data has noise. So the fitting problem is: which point in the column space is closest to Y\mathbf{Y}Y? The closest point on a flat surface to an outside point is the foot of the perpendicular dropped from the point to the surface. That foot is Y^\hat{\mathbf{Y}}Y^, and the perpendicular segment is the residual vector e=Y−Y^\mathbf{e} = \mathbf{Y} - \hat{\mathbf{Y}}e=Y−Y^. Figure 4 draws exactly this for the smallest case that fits on a page, three observations and two columns.

Formula¶

The geometric condition is that the residual is orthogonal to every column of X\mathbf{X}X:

X′e=0,where e=Y−Xb.\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{e} = \mathbf{0}, \qquad \text{where } \mathbf{e} = \mathbf{Y} - \mathbf{X}\mathbf{b} .X′e=0,where e=Y−Xb.

In words: each column of X\mathbf{X}X, dotted with the residual vector, gives zero, so the residual points in a direction the model cannot represent. Expanding X′(Y−Xb)=0\mathbf{X}'(\mathbf{Y} - \mathbf{X}\mathbf{b}) = \mathbf{0}X′(Y−Xb)=0 gives back the normal equations X′Xb=X′Y\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X}\mathbf{b} = \mathbf{X}'\mathbf{Y}X′Xb=X′Y, so the geometry and the calculus lead to the identical solution.

Derivation by projection¶

Derivation (least squares as orthogonal projection). Let b\mathbf{b}b solve the normal equations, so X′e=0\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{e} = \mathbf{0}X′e=0 with e=Y−Xb\mathbf{e} = \mathbf{Y} - \mathbf{X}\mathbf{b}e=Y−Xb. Take any other candidate coefficient vector b∗\mathbf{b}^{\ast}b∗, with fitted vector Xb∗\mathbf{X}\mathbf{b}^{\ast}Xb∗. Split the gap from Y\mathbf{Y}Y to that candidate by routing through Y^=Xb\hat{\mathbf{Y}} = \mathbf{X}\mathbf{b}Y^=Xb:

Y−Xb∗=(Y−Xb)⏟e+X(b−b∗)⏟g.\mathbf{Y} - \mathbf{X}\mathbf{b}^{\ast} = \underbrace{(\mathbf{Y} - \mathbf{X}\mathbf{b})}_{\mathbf{e}} + \underbrace{\mathbf{X}(\mathbf{b} - \mathbf{b}^{\ast})}_{\mathbf{g}} .Y−Xb∗=e(Y−Xb)​​+gX(b−b∗)​​.

The second piece g=X(b−b∗)\mathbf{g} = \mathbf{X}(\mathbf{b} - \mathbf{b}^{\ast})g=X(b−b∗) lives in the column space. Its squared length adds without a cross term, because the cross term vanishes by orthogonality:

e′g=e′X(b−b∗)=(X′e)′(b−b∗)=0′(b−b∗)=0.\mathbf{e}'\mathbf{g} = \mathbf{e}'\mathbf{X}(\mathbf{b} - \mathbf{b}^{\ast}) = (\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{e})'(\mathbf{b} - \mathbf{b}^{\ast}) = \mathbf{0}'(\mathbf{b} - \mathbf{b}^{\ast}) = 0 .e′g=e′X(b−b∗)=(X′e)′(b−b∗)=0′(b−b∗)=0.

Therefore, by the Pythagorean theorem in nnn dimensions,

∥Y−Xb∗∥2=∥e∥2+∥g∥2  ≥  ∥e∥2,\|\mathbf{Y} - \mathbf{X}\mathbf{b}^{\ast}\|^2 = \|\mathbf{e}\|^2 + \|\mathbf{g}\|^2 \; \ge \; \|\mathbf{e}\|^2 ,∥Y−Xb∗∥2=∥e∥2+∥g∥2≥∥e∥2,

with equality only when ∥g∥2=0\|\mathbf{g}\|^2 = 0∥g∥2=0, that is Xb∗=Xb\mathbf{X}\mathbf{b}^{\ast} = \mathbf{X}\mathbf{b}Xb∗=Xb. Since X\mathbf{X}X has full column rank, this forces b∗=b\mathbf{b}^{\ast} = \mathbf{b}b∗=b. So the normal-equations solution gives the unique smallest sum of squared errors, and Y^\hat{\mathbf{Y}}Y^ is the closest point of the column space to Y\mathbf{Y}Y. ■\blacksquare■

This is the same algebraic identity as the Chapter 2 proof in 2.2 Least squares from first principles, now read as geometry: the cross term vanishes there because ∑ei=0\sum e_i = 0∑ei​=0 and ∑Xiei=0\sum X_i e_i = 0∑Xi​ei​=0, which is precisely X′e=0\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{e} = \mathbf{0}X′e=0 written out. The projection view (in 6.5 Idempotent and projection matrices) is the one to remember, because diagnostics in Chapter 9 are all about which observations sit in awkward corners of the column space.

R and Python¶

Example 7.2: The residual is orthogonal to the predictors

Question. Do the Dwaine residuals really satisfy X′e=0\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{e} = \mathbf{0}X′e=0?

Intuition. Compute the fitted vector Y^=Xb\hat{\mathbf{Y}} = \mathbf{X}\mathbf{b}Y^=Xb and the residual e=Y−Y^\mathbf{e} = \mathbf{Y} - \hat{\mathbf{Y}}e=Y−Y^, then form X′e\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{e}X′e. Each of its three entries should be zero (up to floating-point dust), one per column of X\mathbf{X}X.

Formula. e=Y−Xb\mathbf{e} = \mathbf{Y} - \mathbf{X}\mathbf{b}e=Y−Xb and X′e=0\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{e} = \mathbf{0}X′e=0.

Computation.

n <- nrow(X)
p <- ncol(X)
yhat <- X %*% b
e <- Y - yhat
round(t(X) %*% e, 6)          # should be the zero vector
     [,1]
[1,]    0
[2,]    0
[3,]    0
n, p = X.shape
yhat = X @ b
e = Y - yhat
print(np.round(X.T @ e, 6))   # should be the zero vector
[0. 0. 0.]

Interpretation. All three dot products are zero. The first (from the column of ones) is ∑ei=0\sum e_i = 0∑ei​=0; the other two say the residuals are uncorrelated with each predictor. The residual vector carries exactly the part of Y\mathbf{Y}Y that the predictors cannot explain, which is why plotting it against the fitted values is the first diagnostic we draw, in Figure 5.

Durable skill: See a fit as a projection

Once you picture fitting as dropping a perpendicular from the data onto the space of possible fits, a lot of regression stops needing memorization. The residual is orthogonal to the fit because the perpendicular is the shortest route. Adding a predictor enlarges the column space, so the residual can only get shorter. Two predictors that point in nearly the same direction span a thin sliver of space, which is why their coefficients are unstable. You will meet this picture again in the leverage values (Chapter 9), in added-variable plots (8.5 Added-variable plots), and in any method that decomposes data into “fit plus leftover.” To practice, next time you fit a model, ask what space the fit lives in and what direction the residual points.

7.3 The hat matrix¶

Intuition¶

Section 7.2 pictured the fit as a projection of Y\mathbf{Y}Y onto the column space. That projection is carried out by a single matrix, and giving it a name pays off for the rest of the chapter. The fitted vector is a fixed linear function of the data: plug the formula for b\mathbf{b}b into Y^=Xb\hat{\mathbf{Y}} = \mathbf{X}\mathbf{b}Y^=Xb and you get Y^=X(X′X)−1X′Y\hat{\mathbf{Y}} = \mathbf{X}(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{Y}Y^=X(X′X)−1X′Y. The matrix sitting in front of Y\mathbf{Y}Y turns the raw responses into fitted values, so it earns the name hat matrix (Definition 7.6): it puts the hat on Y\mathbf{Y}Y. It is the single most useful matrix in the rest of the course. Its diagonal entries are the leverages that flag unusual predictor values (Chapter 9), and its algebra makes the variance results below fall out in a line.

Formula¶

Definition 7.6: Hat matrix

The hat matrix is the n×nn \times nn×n matrix

H=X(X′X)−1X′,\mathbf{H} = \mathbf{X}(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}',H=X(X′X)−1X′,

which depends only on the predictors and maps the responses to the fitted values, Y^=HY\hat{\mathbf{Y}} = \mathbf{H}\mathbf{Y}Y^=HY: it puts the hat on Y\mathbf{Y}Y.

The hat matrix and the fitted and residual vectors are

H=X(X′X)−1X′,Y^=HY,e=(I−H)Y.\mathbf{H} = \mathbf{X}(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}', \qquad \hat{\mathbf{Y}} = \mathbf{H}\mathbf{Y}, \qquad \mathbf{e} = (\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{H})\mathbf{Y} .H=X(X′X)−1X′,Y^=HY,e=(I−H)Y.

  • H\mathbf{H}H is n×nn \times nn×n and depends only on the predictors, not on Y\mathbf{Y}Y.

  • Y^=HY\hat{\mathbf{Y}} = \mathbf{H}\mathbf{Y}Y^=HY: each fitted value is a fixed weighted average of all the responses, with weights read off a row of H\mathbf{H}H.

  • e=(I−H)Y\mathbf{e} = (\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{H})\mathbf{Y}e=(I−H)Y: the residual is what is left after projecting, so I−H\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{H}I−H is itself a projection, onto the space perpendicular to the columns of X\mathbf{X}X.

Figure 6 shows the Toluca hat matrix as a grid of weights; the diagonal entries, outlined, are the leverages hiih_{ii}hii​.

Derivation (properties of H)¶

Theorem 7.7: Properties of the hat matrix

For a design matrix X\mathbf{X}X of full column rank, the hat matrix H=X(X′X)−1X′\mathbf{H} = \mathbf{X}(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}'H=X(X′X)−1X′ is symmetric (H′=H\mathbf{H}' = \mathbf{H}H′=H) and idempotent (HH=H\mathbf{H}\mathbf{H} = \mathbf{H}HH=H), with tr⁡(H)=p\operatorname{tr}(\mathbf{H}) = ptr(H)=p. Consequently I−H\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{H}I−H is also symmetric and idempotent, with tr⁡(I−H)=n−p\operatorname{tr}(\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{H}) = n - ptr(I−H)=n−p.

Proof. Write A=(X′X)−1\mathbf{A} = (\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}A=(X′X)−1, which is symmetric because X′X\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X}X′X is (the inverse of a symmetric matrix is symmetric, by 6.4 The inverse and the normal equations).

Symmetric. Using (BCD)′=D′C′B′(\mathbf{BCD})' = \mathbf{D}'\mathbf{C}'\mathbf{B}'(BCD)′=D′C′B′ from 6.2 Transpose and matrix multiplication,

H′=(XAX′)′=XA′X′=XAX′=H.\mathbf{H}' = \big(\mathbf{X}\mathbf{A}\mathbf{X}'\big)' = \mathbf{X}\mathbf{A}'\mathbf{X}' = \mathbf{X}\mathbf{A}\mathbf{X}' = \mathbf{H} .H′=(XAX′)′=XA′X′=XAX′=H.

Idempotent. Projecting twice equals projecting once. The middle X′X\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X}X′X cancels its inverse:

HH=XA X′X AX′=XAX′=H.\mathbf{H}\mathbf{H} = \mathbf{X}\mathbf{A}\,\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X}\,\mathbf{A}\mathbf{X}' = \mathbf{X}\mathbf{A}\mathbf{X}' = \mathbf{H} .HH=XAX′XAX′=XAX′=H.

Figure 7 shows why: once H\mathbf{H}H has landed a vector in the column space, projecting again cannot move it.

Trace ppp. The trace of a matrix is the sum of its diagonal entries. Use the cyclic property of the trace, tr⁡(BC)=tr⁡(CB)\operatorname{tr}(\mathbf{B}\mathbf{C}) = \operatorname{tr}(\mathbf{C}\mathbf{B})tr(BC)=tr(CB) (a standard identity: both sides equal ∑i,jBijCji\sum_{i,j} B_{ij} C_{ji}∑i,j​Bij​Cji​), to slide X\mathbf{X}X around:

tr⁡(H)=tr⁡ ⁣(XAX′)=tr⁡ ⁣(AX′X)=tr⁡ ⁣((X′X)−1(X′X))=tr⁡(Ip)=p.\operatorname{tr}(\mathbf{H}) = \operatorname{tr}\!\big(\mathbf{X}\mathbf{A}\mathbf{X}'\big) = \operatorname{tr}\!\big(\mathbf{A}\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X}\big) = \operatorname{tr}\!\big((\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})\big) = \operatorname{tr}(\mathbf{I}_p) = p .tr(H)=tr(XAX′)=tr(AX′X)=tr((X′X)−1(X′X))=tr(Ip​)=p.

Consequently I−H\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{H}I−H is also symmetric and idempotent, with tr⁡(I−H)=n−p\operatorname{tr}(\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{H}) = n - ptr(I−H)=n−p. That trace is the degrees of freedom for error, and it is the reason the divisor in the variance estimate is n−pn - pn−p, proved in 7.5 Estimating the error variance. ■\blacksquare■

Key idea

Almost every result left in this chapter rests on two facts about the hat matrix: it is a projection (symmetric and idempotent), and its trace counts the parameters, so tr⁡H=p\operatorname{tr}\mathbf{H} = ptrH=p and tr⁡(I−H)=n−p\operatorname{tr}(\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{H}) = n - ptr(I−H)=n−p. The first fact makes the fitted values and residuals behave; the second fixes the divisor in the variance estimate. Hold those two and the rest is bookkeeping.

R and Python¶

Example 7.3: Checking the hat matrix properties

Question. For the Dwaine design, is H\mathbf{H}H symmetric and idempotent, and is its trace equal to p=3p = 3p=3?

Intuition. Build H\mathbf{H}H from X\mathbf{X}X, compare it to its transpose and to HH\mathbf{H}\mathbf{H}HH, and sum its diagonal.

Formula. H=X(X′X)−1X′\mathbf{H} = \mathbf{X}(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}'H=X(X′X)−1X′, H′=H\mathbf{H}' = \mathbf{H}H′=H, HH=H\mathbf{H}\mathbf{H} = \mathbf{H}HH=H, tr⁡(H)=p\operatorname{tr}(\mathbf{H}) = ptr(H)=p.

Computation.

H <- X %*% XtX_inv %*% t(X)
c(symmetric = all.equal(H, t(H)),
  idempotent = all.equal(H %*% H, H))
sum(diag(H))                  # trace of H
 symmetric idempotent
      TRUE       TRUE
[1] 3
H = X @ np.linalg.inv(XtX) @ X.T
print(np.allclose(H, H.T), np.allclose(H @ H, H))
print(round(np.trace(H), 6))  # trace of H
True True
3.0

Interpretation. The hat matrix is symmetric and idempotent, and its trace is exactly 3=p3 = p3=p. The trace being ppp is not a coincidence of these data: it holds for any full-rank design, and it is the fact that makes MSE\mathrm{MSE}MSE unbiased. The 21×2121 \times 2121×21 matrix H\mathbf{H}H compresses to a single meaningful number, its trace, which counts the parameters the fit spends.

Try it 7.2

The Toluca simple-regression model has n=25n = 25n=25 and p=2p = 2p=2. Using only the trace property, state tr⁡(H)\operatorname{tr}(\mathbf{H})tr(H) and tr⁡(I−H)\operatorname{tr}(\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{H})tr(I−H), and say what each counts.

Solution

tr⁡(H)=p=2\operatorname{tr}(\mathbf{H}) = p = 2tr(H)=p=2, the number of parameters (intercept and slope), which is the dimension of the column space the fit lives in. tr⁡(I−H)=n−p=25−2=23\operatorname{tr}(\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{H}) = n - p = 25 - 2 = 23tr(I−H)=n−p=25−2=23, the error degrees of freedom, the dimension of the space the residual lives in. The two spaces are perpendicular and their dimensions add to n=25n = 25n=25.

7.4 The sampling distribution of b¶

Intuition¶

The vector b\mathbf{b}b came from one sample of 21 cities. A different 21 cities would give a slightly different b\mathbf{b}b, so b\mathbf{b}b is random, with a mean and a covariance across the samples we could have drawn. Two questions repeat from Chapter 2, now for a whole vector: is b\mathbf{b}b right on average, and how much does it vary? The answers are clean because b\mathbf{b}b is a fixed matrix times Y\mathbf{Y}Y, and 6.7 Random vectors, expectation, and covariance matrices tells us exactly how means and covariances pass through a linear map.

Formula¶

The least-squares vector is unbiased with a covariance matrix built from (X′X)−1(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}(X′X)−1:

E{b}=β,Cov⁡{b}=σ2(X′X)−1.E\{\mathbf{b}\} = \boldsymbol{\beta}, \qquad \operatorname{Cov}\{\mathbf{b}\} = \sigma^2 (\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1} .E{b}=β,Cov{b}=σ2(X′X)−1.

In words: on average b\mathbf{b}b hits the true coefficient vector, and its covariance matrix is σ2\sigma^2σ2 times the inverse cross-product matrix. The diagonal entries are the variances of the individual coefficients; the off-diagonal entries are the covariances between them, which is why two coefficients can be correlated (the tilted cloud in Figure 8). Replacing σ2\sigma^2σ2 by its estimate MSE\mathrm{MSE}MSE gives the estimated covariance matrix s2{b}=MSE (X′X)−1s^2\{\mathbf{b}\} = \mathrm{MSE}\,(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}s2{b}=MSE(X′X)−1, whose diagonal square roots are the standard errors software prints.

Derivation (mean and covariance of b)¶

Theorem 7.8: Mean and covariance of the least-squares estimator

Under the general linear model with E{ε}=0E\{\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}\} = \mathbf{0}E{ε}=0 and Cov⁡{ε}=σ2I\operatorname{Cov}\{\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}\} = \sigma^2\mathbf{I}Cov{ε}=σ2I, the least-squares estimator is unbiased with covariance

E{b}=β,Cov⁡{b}=σ2(X′X)−1.E\{\mathbf{b}\} = \boldsymbol{\beta}, \qquad \operatorname{Cov}\{\mathbf{b}\} = \sigma^2 (\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1} .E{b}=β,Cov{b}=σ2(X′X)−1.

If in addition ε∼N(0,σ2I)\boldsymbol{\varepsilon} \sim N(\mathbf{0}, \sigma^2\mathbf{I})ε∼N(0,σ2I), then b∼N ⁣(β, σ2(X′X)−1)\mathbf{b} \sim N\!\big(\boldsymbol{\beta},\ \sigma^2 (\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}\big)b∼N(β, σ2(X′X)−1).

Proof. Write b=AY\mathbf{b} = \mathbf{A}\mathbf{Y}b=AY with the fixed matrix A=(X′X)−1X′\mathbf{A} = (\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}'A=(X′X)−1X′. For a fixed matrix times a random vector, 6.7 Random vectors, expectation, and covariance matrices gives E{AY}=A E{Y}E\{\mathbf{A}\mathbf{Y}\} = \mathbf{A}\,E\{\mathbf{Y}\}E{AY}=AE{Y} and Cov⁡{AY}=ACov⁡{Y}A′\operatorname{Cov}\{\mathbf{A}\mathbf{Y}\} = \mathbf{A}\operatorname{Cov}\{\mathbf{Y}\}\mathbf{A}'Cov{AY}=ACov{Y}A′.

Mean. Since E{Y}=XβE\{\mathbf{Y}\} = \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta}E{Y}=Xβ,

E{b}=(X′X)−1X′ Xβ=(X′X)−1(X′X)β=β.E\{\mathbf{b}\} = (\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}'\,\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta} = (\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})\boldsymbol{\beta} = \boldsymbol{\beta} .E{b}=(X′X)−1X′Xβ=(X′X)−1(X′X)β=β.

Covariance. Since Cov⁡{Y}=σ2I\operatorname{Cov}\{\mathbf{Y}\} = \sigma^2\mathbf{I}Cov{Y}=σ2I,

Cov⁡{b}=A (σ2I) A′=σ2(X′X)−1X′ X(X′X)−1=σ2(X′X)−1,\operatorname{Cov}\{\mathbf{b}\} = \mathbf{A}\,(\sigma^2\mathbf{I})\,\mathbf{A}' = \sigma^2 (\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}'\,\mathbf{X}(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1} = \sigma^2 (\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1} ,Cov{b}=A(σ2I)A′=σ2(X′X)−1X′X(X′X)−1=σ2(X′X)−1,

where the middle X′X\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X}X′X cancels one inverse and the symmetry of (X′X)−1(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}(X′X)−1 handles the transpose. ■\blacksquare■

If we add the normality assumption ε∼N(0,σ2I)\boldsymbol{\varepsilon} \sim N(\mathbf{0}, \sigma^2\mathbf{I})ε∼N(0,σ2I), then Y\mathbf{Y}Y is multivariate normal, and a fixed linear map of a multivariate normal is again multivariate normal (6.8 The multivariate normal, in brief). So

b∼N ⁣(β, σ2(X′X)−1).\mathbf{b} \sim N\!\big(\boldsymbol{\beta},\ \sigma^2 (\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}\big) .b∼N(β, σ2(X′X)−1).

In words: under normal errors each coefficient is normally distributed around its true value, and this is what powers the ttt tests and confidence intervals of Chapter 8. Figure 8 confirms the covariance by simulation: refitting many datasets scatters the two Dwaine slopes into a tilted ellipse that matches σ2(X′X)−1\sigma^2 (\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}σ2(X′X)−1.

R and Python¶

Example 7.4: Standard errors from the covariance matrix

Question. How precisely are the three Dwaine coefficients estimated, and do the matrix-formula standard errors match what lm and statsmodels print?

Intuition. Multiply (X′X)−1(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}(X′X)−1 by MSE\mathrm{MSE}MSE and take the square roots of the diagonal. (We compute MSE\mathrm{MSE}MSE itself in 7.5 Estimating the error variance; here we use it.)

Formula. s2{b}=MSE (X′X)−1s^2\{\mathbf{b}\} = \mathrm{MSE}\,(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}s2{b}=MSE(X′X)−1, and s{bj}=[ s2{b} ]jjs\{b_j\} = \sqrt{[\,s^2\{\mathbf{b}\}\,]_{jj}}s{bj​}=[s2{b}]jj​​.

Computation.

SSE <- sum(e^2)
MSE <- SSE / (n - p)
cov_b <- MSE * XtX_inv
se_b <- sqrt(diag(cov_b))
round(se_b, 4)
[1] 60.0170  0.2118  4.0640
SSE = np.sum(e ** 2)
MSE = SSE / (n - p)
cov_b = MSE * np.linalg.inv(XtX)
se_b = np.sqrt(np.diag(cov_b))
print(np.round(se_b, 4))
[60.017   0.2118  4.064 ]

The built-in summaries print the same standard errors in their “Std. Error” column.

summary(lm(sales ~ targtpop + dispoinc, data = dwaine))$coefficients
             Estimate Std. Error   t value     Pr(>|t|)
(Intercept) -68.85707 60.0169532 -1.147294 2.662817e-01
targtpop      1.45456  0.2117817  6.868201 2.001691e-06
dispoinc      9.36550  4.0639581  2.304527 3.332136e-02

Interpretation. The standard errors are 60.02, 0.212, and 4.064 in the coefficients’ own units. The population slope 1.4546 has a standard error of only 0.212, so it is pinned down tightly (its ttt value near 6.9 is far from zero); the income slope 9.37 with standard error 4.06 is less certain; the intercept is loose, as intercepts far from the data usually are. Every number matches the built-in output to all printed digits, because the built-in output is computing this exact matrix formula.

Key idea

The mean E{b}=βE\{\mathbf{b}\} = \boldsymbol{\beta}E{b}=β, the covariance σ2(X′X)−1\sigma^2(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}σ2(X′X)−1, and the Gauss-Markov optimality all hold with no normality assumption: mean-zero, equal-variance, uncorrelated errors are enough. Normality is a separate, stronger claim. It buys the exact normal sampling distribution of b\mathbf{b}b, and with it the ttt and FFF tests of Chapter 8.

7.5 Estimating the error variance¶

Intuition¶

The coefficients describe the plane; the model still has one more unknown, the error variance σ2\sigma^2σ2, which sets how far the data scatter off the plane. As in Chapter 2 we estimate it from the residuals, but now we must find the right divisor for a model with ppp parameters instead of 2. The answer is n−pn - pn−p, and the reason is the trace we computed in 7.3 The hat matrix: fitting ppp coefficients uses up ppp directions of the data, leaving n−pn - pn−p directions for the residual to live in. Figure 9 keeps the bookkeeping in view: the nnn directions of the data split into a fit part and a residual part, and we average the squared residual over its own n−pn - pn−p directions, not all nnn.

Formula¶

The error sum of squares and the estimator of σ2\sigma^2σ2 are

SSE=e′e=Y′(I−H)Y,s2=MSE=SSEn−p,s=MSE.\mathrm{SSE} = \mathbf{e}'\mathbf{e} = \mathbf{Y}'(\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{H})\mathbf{Y}, \qquad s^2 = \mathrm{MSE} = \frac{\mathrm{SSE}}{n - p}, \qquad s = \sqrt{\mathrm{MSE}} .SSE=e′e=Y′(I−H)Y,s2=MSE=n−pSSE​,s=MSE​.

  • SSE\mathrm{SSE}SSE is the squared length of the residual vector, the variation the model did not explain.

  • MSE\mathrm{MSE}MSE divides SSE\mathrm{SSE}SSE by its degrees of freedom n−pn - pn−p, and estimates σ2\sigma^2σ2.

  • sss is the estimated error standard deviation, in the units of YYY.

Derivation (why the divisor is n - p)¶

Theorem 7.9: Unbiasedness of MSE

Under the general linear model with Cov⁡{ε}=σ2I\operatorname{Cov}\{\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}\} = \sigma^2\mathbf{I}Cov{ε}=σ2I and X\mathbf{X}X of full column rank,

E{SSE}=(n−p)σ2,soE{MSE}=σ2.E\{\mathrm{SSE}\} = (n - p)\sigma^2, \qquad \text{so} \qquad E\{\mathrm{MSE}\} = \sigma^2 .E{SSE}=(n−p)σ2,soE{MSE}=σ2.

The divisor n−pn - pn−p, and no other, makes MSE=SSE/(n−p)\mathrm{MSE} = \mathrm{SSE}/(n - p)MSE=SSE/(n−p) an unbiased estimator of the error variance σ2\sigma^2σ2.

We need one fact about the expected value of a quadratic form. It is provable at our level, so we prove it, then apply it.

Lemma (expectation of a quadratic form). For any random vector Y\mathbf{Y}Y with mean μ\boldsymbol{\mu}μ and covariance Σ\boldsymbol{\Sigma}Σ, and any fixed symmetric matrix A\mathbf{A}A,

E{Y′AY}=tr⁡(AΣ)+μ′Aμ.E\{\mathbf{Y}'\mathbf{A}\mathbf{Y}\} = \operatorname{tr}(\mathbf{A}\boldsymbol{\Sigma}) + \boldsymbol{\mu}'\mathbf{A}\boldsymbol{\mu} .E{Y′AY}=tr(AΣ)+μ′Aμ.

Proof. The scalar Y′AY\mathbf{Y}'\mathbf{A}\mathbf{Y}Y′AY equals its own trace, and the trace is cyclic, so Y′AY=tr⁡(AYY′)\mathbf{Y}'\mathbf{A}\mathbf{Y} = \operatorname{tr}(\mathbf{A}\mathbf{Y}\mathbf{Y}')Y′AY=tr(AYY′). Taking expectations and using E{YY′}=Σ+μμ′E\{\mathbf{Y}\mathbf{Y}'\} = \boldsymbol{\Sigma} + \boldsymbol{\mu}\boldsymbol{\mu}'E{YY′}=Σ+μμ′ (6.7 Random vectors, expectation, and covariance matrices),

E{Y′AY}=tr⁡ ⁣(A(Σ+μμ′))=tr⁡(AΣ)+tr⁡(Aμμ′)=tr⁡(AΣ)+μ′Aμ,E\{\mathbf{Y}'\mathbf{A}\mathbf{Y}\} = \operatorname{tr}\!\big(\mathbf{A}(\boldsymbol{\Sigma} + \boldsymbol{\mu}\boldsymbol{\mu}')\big) = \operatorname{tr}(\mathbf{A}\boldsymbol{\Sigma}) + \operatorname{tr}(\mathbf{A}\boldsymbol{\mu}\boldsymbol{\mu}') = \operatorname{tr}(\mathbf{A}\boldsymbol{\Sigma}) + \boldsymbol{\mu}'\mathbf{A}\boldsymbol{\mu} ,E{Y′AY}=tr(A(Σ+μμ′))=tr(AΣ)+tr(Aμμ′)=tr(AΣ)+μ′Aμ,

since tr⁡(Aμμ′)=μ′Aμ\operatorname{tr}(\mathbf{A}\boldsymbol{\mu}\boldsymbol{\mu}') = \boldsymbol{\mu}'\mathbf{A}\boldsymbol{\mu}tr(Aμμ′)=μ′Aμ is a scalar. ■\blacksquare■

Proof of Theorem 7.9. Apply the lemma with A=I−H\mathbf{A} = \mathbf{I} - \mathbf{H}A=I−H, μ=Xβ\boldsymbol{\mu} = \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta}μ=Xβ, and Σ=σ2I\boldsymbol{\Sigma} = \sigma^2\mathbf{I}Σ=σ2I. The two pieces are:

Trace piece. tr⁡ ⁣((I−H)σ2I)=σ2tr⁡(I−H)=σ2(n−p)\operatorname{tr}\!\big((\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{H})\sigma^2\mathbf{I}\big) = \sigma^2 \operatorname{tr}(\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{H}) = \sigma^2 (n - p)tr((I−H)σ2I)=σ2tr(I−H)=σ2(n−p), using the trace from 7.3 The hat matrix.

Mean piece. The mean term vanishes, because (I−H)X=X−HX=X−X=0(\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{H})\mathbf{X} = \mathbf{X} - \mathbf{H}\mathbf{X} = \mathbf{X} - \mathbf{X} = \mathbf{0}(I−H)X=X−HX=X−X=0 (the hat matrix leaves the columns of X\mathbf{X}X unchanged, since they already lie in the column space). Therefore

μ′(I−H)μ=β′X′(I−H)Xβ=β′0 β=0.\boldsymbol{\mu}'(\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{H})\boldsymbol{\mu} = \boldsymbol{\beta}'\mathbf{X}'(\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{H})\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta} = \boldsymbol{\beta}'\mathbf{0}\,\boldsymbol{\beta} = 0 .μ′(I−H)μ=β′X′(I−H)Xβ=β′0β=0.

Combining, E{SSE}=σ2(n−p)+0=(n−p)σ2E\{\mathrm{SSE}\} = \sigma^2(n - p) + 0 = (n - p)\sigma^2E{SSE}=σ2(n−p)+0=(n−p)σ2, so

E{MSE}=E{SSE}n−p=σ2.E\{\mathrm{MSE}\} = \frac{E\{\mathrm{SSE}\}}{n - p} = \sigma^2 .E{MSE}=n−pE{SSE}​=σ2.

The divisor n−pn - pn−p, and no other, makes MSE\mathrm{MSE}MSE unbiased. ■\blacksquare■

This is the Chapter 2 result E{MSE}=σ2E\{\mathrm{MSE}\} = \sigma^2E{MSE}=σ2 generalized: there p=2p = 2p=2 gave the divisor n−2n - 2n−2; here any model divides by nnn minus the number of parameters. Figure 10 shows what goes wrong with the naive divisor nnn: dividing by nnn instead of n−pn - pn−p biases the estimate low, and the gap is largest when ppp is a big fraction of nnn.

R and Python¶

Example 7.5: The error variance for Dwaine, and Toluca as a check

Question. What is MSE\mathrm{MSE}MSE for the Dwaine model, and does the matrix machinery reproduce the Toluca MSE\mathrm{MSE}MSE from Chapter 2 exactly?

Intuition. For Dwaine, sum the squared residuals and divide by n−p=18n - p = 18n−p=18. Then run the whole matrix pipeline on Toluca (p=2p = 2p=2) and confirm every number matches 2.2 Least squares from first principles.

Formula. MSE=SSE/(n−p)\mathrm{MSE} = \mathrm{SSE}/(n - p)MSE=SSE/(n−p); for Toluca n=25n = 25n=25, p=2p = 2p=2.

Computation.

c(n = n, p = p, SSE = round(SSE, 3), MSE = round(MSE, 4), s = round(sqrt(MSE), 4))
        n         p       SSE       MSE         s
  21.0000    3.0000 2180.9270  121.1626   11.0074
print(n, p, round(SSE, 3), round(MSE, 4), round(np.sqrt(MSE), 4))
21 3 2180.927 121.1626 11.0074

Now the Toluca refit through the identical matrix formulas:

toluca <- read.csv("data/toluca.csv")
Xt <- cbind(1, toluca$lotsize)
Yt <- toluca$hours
XtXt_inv <- solve(t(Xt) %*% Xt)
bt <- XtXt_inv %*% t(Xt) %*% Yt
et <- Yt - Xt %*% bt
MSEt <- sum(et^2) / (nrow(Xt) - 2)
se_t <- sqrt(diag(MSEt * XtXt_inv))
round(c(b0 = bt[1], b1 = bt[2], MSE = MSEt,
        se_b0 = se_t[1], se_b1 = se_t[2]), 4)
       b0        b1       MSE     se_b0     se_b1
  62.3659    3.5702 2383.7156   26.1774    0.3470
toluca = pd.read_csv("data/toluca.csv")
Xt = np.column_stack([np.ones(len(toluca)), toluca["lotsize"]])
Yt = toluca["hours"].to_numpy()
XtXt_inv = np.linalg.inv(Xt.T @ Xt)
bt = XtXt_inv @ Xt.T @ Yt
et = Yt - Xt @ bt
MSEt = np.sum(et ** 2) / (len(toluca) - 2)
se_t = np.sqrt(np.diag(MSEt * XtXt_inv))
print(round(bt[0], 4), round(bt[1], 4), round(MSEt, 4),
      round(se_t[0], 4), round(se_t[1], 4))
62.3659 3.5702 2383.7156 26.1774 0.347

Interpretation. For Dwaine, MSE=121.16\mathrm{MSE} = 121.16MSE=121.16 and s=11.01s = 11.01s=11.01 thousand dollars: even knowing a city’s population and income, the model’s sales prediction is typically off by about $11{,}000. For Toluca the matrix pipeline returns b0=62.3659b_0 = 62.3659b0​=62.3659, b1=3.5702b_1 = 3.5702b1​=3.5702, MSE=2383.72\mathrm{MSE} = 2383.72MSE=2383.72, s{b0}=26.1774s\{b_0\} = 26.1774s{b0​}=26.1774, and s{b1}=0.3470s\{b_1\} = 0.3470s{b1​}=0.3470, matching 2.2 Least squares from first principles (and 2.5 Sampling behavior and the Gauss-Markov theorem for the standard errors) to every digit shown. The two-parameter machinery of Chapter 2 and the general machinery here are the same method; 7.7 Simple regression as a special case shows the algebra of why.

Try it 7.3

The Dwaine model has n=21n = 21n=21, p=3p = 3p=3, SSE=2180.93\mathrm{SSE} = 2180.93SSE=2180.93. A classmate reports s2=2180.93/21=103.9s^2 = 2180.93 / 21 = 103.9s2=2180.93/21=103.9. Which quantity did they compute, is it too large or too small as an estimate of σ2\sigma^2σ2, and what is the correct MSE\mathrm{MSE}MSE?

Solution

They divided by nnn instead of n−pn - pn−p, which is the maximum-likelihood variance estimate, biased low because it ignores the three parameters spent on the fit. The correct estimate is MSE=2180.93/(21−3)=2180.93/18=121.16\mathrm{MSE} = 2180.93 / (21 - 3) = 2180.93 / 18 = 121.16MSE=2180.93/(21−3)=2180.93/18=121.16. The classmate’s 103.9 understates σ2\sigma^2σ2; it matches the biased mean in Figure 10.

We can also read off how well the plane fits overall. The total variation SSTO=∑(Yi−Yˉ)2\mathrm{SSTO} = \sum (Y_i - \bar{Y})^2SSTO=∑(Yi​−Yˉ)2 splits into the part the model explains and the part it does not, and R2=1−SSE/SSTOR^2 = 1 - \mathrm{SSE}/\mathrm{SSTO}R2=1−SSE/SSTO reports the explained fraction, exactly as in 3.6 The analysis of variance approach but now for many predictors.

SSTO <- sum((Y - mean(Y))^2)
SSR <- SSTO - SSE
c(SSTO = round(SSTO, 2), SSR = round(SSR, 2), SSE = round(SSE, 2),
  R2 = round(1 - SSE / SSTO, 4))
      SSTO        SSR        SSE         R2
26196.2100 24015.2800  2180.9300     0.9167
SSTO = np.sum((Y - Y.mean()) ** 2)
SSR = SSTO - SSE
print(round(SSTO, 2), round(SSR, 2), round(SSE, 2), round(1 - SSE / SSTO, 4))
26196.21 24015.28 2180.93 0.9167

The two predictors together explain about 91.7%91.7\%91.7% of the variation in Dwaine sales. Chapter 8 unpacks this number, warns about how it can only rise when predictors are added, and builds the general linear test on top of the SSE\mathrm{SSE}SSE we just computed (8.3 Extra sums of squares).

7.6 The Gauss-Markov theorem¶

Intuition¶

We chose least squares because squared error is a natural penalty, and Chapter 2 rewarded that choice: in simple regression, no other linear unbiased estimator beats b1b_1b1​. The same reward holds for the full vector, and the matrix proof is barely longer than the scalar one. The claim is that any competitor built as a fixed linear combination of the responses, and required to be right on average, must have a covariance matrix at least as large as b\mathbf{b}b's. No normality is needed; the mean-zero, constant-variance, uncorrelated error assumptions are enough. Figure 11 shows the payoff on Toluca: least squares and a competing unbiased estimator are both centered on the truth, but least squares scatters far less.

Formula¶

Theorem 7.10: Gauss-Markov theorem

Under the model Y=Xβ+ε\mathbf{Y} = \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta} + \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}Y=Xβ+ε with E{ε}=0E\{\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}\} = \mathbf{0}E{ε}=0 and Cov⁡{ε}=σ2I\operatorname{Cov}\{\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}\} = \sigma^2\mathbf{I}Cov{ε}=σ2I, and X\mathbf{X}X of full column rank, the least-squares estimator b=(X′X)−1X′Y\mathbf{b} = (\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{Y}b=(X′X)−1X′Y is the best linear unbiased estimator of β\boldsymbol{\beta}β: for any fixed vector c\mathbf{c}c, the estimator c′b\mathbf{c}'\mathbf{b}c′b has the smallest variance among all estimators that are linear in Y\mathbf{Y}Y and unbiased for c′β\mathbf{c}'\boldsymbol{\beta}c′β.

Derivation (b is BLUE)¶

Proof. Let b∗=CY\mathbf{b}^{\ast} = \mathbf{C}\mathbf{Y}b∗=CY be any linear estimator with a fixed p×np \times np×n matrix C\mathbf{C}C. Its mean is E{CY}=CXβE\{\mathbf{C}\mathbf{Y}\} = \mathbf{C}\mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta}E{CY}=CXβ. For b∗\mathbf{b}^{\ast}b∗ to be unbiased for β\boldsymbol{\beta}β for every possible β\boldsymbol{\beta}β, we need

CX=Ip.\mathbf{C}\mathbf{X} = \mathbf{I}_p .CX=Ip​.

Write C=(X′X)−1X′+D\mathbf{C} = (\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}' + \mathbf{D}C=(X′X)−1X′+D, defining D\mathbf{D}D as the difference from the least-squares matrix. The unbiasedness condition CX=I\mathbf{C}\mathbf{X} = \mathbf{I}CX=I becomes

I=[(X′X)−1X′+D]X=I+DX,soDX=0.\mathbf{I} = \big[(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}' + \mathbf{D}\big]\mathbf{X} = \mathbf{I} + \mathbf{D}\mathbf{X}, \qquad \text{so} \qquad \mathbf{D}\mathbf{X} = \mathbf{0} .I=[(X′X)−1X′+D]X=I+DX,soDX=0.

Now compute the covariance. Since Cov⁡{Y}=σ2I\operatorname{Cov}\{\mathbf{Y}\} = \sigma^2\mathbf{I}Cov{Y}=σ2I, Cov⁡{b∗}=σ2CC′\operatorname{Cov}\{\mathbf{b}^{\ast}\} = \sigma^2\mathbf{C}\mathbf{C}'Cov{b∗}=σ2CC′. Expand CC′\mathbf{C}\mathbf{C}'CC′ with C=(X′X)−1X′+D\mathbf{C} = (\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}' + \mathbf{D}C=(X′X)−1X′+D. The two cross terms vanish because DX=0\mathbf{D}\mathbf{X} = \mathbf{0}DX=0 (and its transpose X′D′=0\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{D}' = \mathbf{0}X′D′=0):

(X′X)−1X′D′=(X′X)−1(DX)′=0.(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{D}' = (\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}(\mathbf{D}\mathbf{X})' = \mathbf{0} .(X′X)−1X′D′=(X′X)−1(DX)′=0.

What survives is

CC′=(X′X)−1X′X(X′X)−1+DD′=(X′X)−1+DD′.\mathbf{C}\mathbf{C}' = (\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X}(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1} + \mathbf{D}\mathbf{D}' = (\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1} + \mathbf{D}\mathbf{D}' .CC′=(X′X)−1X′X(X′X)−1+DD′=(X′X)−1+DD′.

Therefore

Cov⁡{b∗}=σ2(X′X)−1+σ2DD′=Cov⁡{b}+σ2DD′.\operatorname{Cov}\{\mathbf{b}^{\ast}\} = \sigma^2 (\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1} + \sigma^2\mathbf{D}\mathbf{D}' = \operatorname{Cov}\{\mathbf{b}\} + \sigma^2\mathbf{D}\mathbf{D}' .Cov{b∗}=σ2(X′X)−1+σ2DD′=Cov{b}+σ2DD′.

For any fixed c\mathbf{c}c, the variance of the scalar c′b∗\mathbf{c}'\mathbf{b}^{\ast}c′b∗ is

Var⁡{c′b∗}=c′Cov⁡{b}c+σ2c′DD′c=Var⁡{c′b}+σ2∥D′c∥2.\operatorname{Var}\{\mathbf{c}'\mathbf{b}^{\ast}\} = \mathbf{c}'\operatorname{Cov}\{\mathbf{b}\}\mathbf{c} + \sigma^2 \mathbf{c}'\mathbf{D}\mathbf{D}'\mathbf{c} = \operatorname{Var}\{\mathbf{c}'\mathbf{b}\} + \sigma^2\|\mathbf{D}'\mathbf{c}\|^2 .Var{c′b∗}=c′Cov{b}c+σ2c′DD′c=Var{c′b}+σ2∥D′c∥2.

The added term σ2∥D′c∥2≥0\sigma^2\|\mathbf{D}'\mathbf{c}\|^2 \ge 0σ2∥D′c∥2≥0 is a squared length, so every linear unbiased competitor has variance at least that of c′b\mathbf{c}'\mathbf{b}c′b, with equality only when D′c=0\mathbf{D}'\mathbf{c} = \mathbf{0}D′c=0; taking c\mathbf{c}c to range over the coordinate vectors forces D=0\mathbf{D} = \mathbf{0}D=0, that is, the competitor is b\mathbf{b}b itself. Least squares is BLUE. ■\blacksquare■

Setting X=[1,x]\mathbf{X} = [\mathbf{1}, \mathbf{x}]X=[1,x] and c=(0,1)′\mathbf{c} = (0, 1)'c=(0,1)′ recovers the Chapter 2 statement that b1b_1b1​ is BLUE (2.5 Sampling behavior and the Gauss-Markov theorem); the matrix proof did every coefficient, and every linear combination of coefficients, at once.

Durable skill: Prove the general case, use it forever

The scalar Gauss-Markov proof in Chapter 2 took a page and covered one estimator. The matrix proof here is about the same length and covers every linear regression you will ever fit, with any number of predictors, plus every linear combination of coefficients (predictions included). That is the payoff of the right notation: a single argument at the general level retires an unbounded list of special cases. When you find yourself proving the same kind of thing twice, look for the notation that lets you prove it once. To practice, take a result you know for two predictors and rewrite its proof so that nothing in it mentions the number two.

7.7 Simple regression as a special case¶

Intuition¶

Everything in Chapters 2 and 3 should drop out of the matrix formulas when there is one predictor, and it does. Seeing it happen once turns the general formulas from abstractions into trusted tools: the same machine that fit Dwaine’s plane fits Toluca’s line, and returns the exact numbers you already know. Figure 12 is the whole idea in one picture: one formula, two jobs.

Derivation (matrix formulas reduce to Chapter 2)¶

Derivation (the p=2p = 2p=2 case). With one predictor, the design matrix is X=[1,x]\mathbf{X} = [\mathbf{1}, \mathbf{x}]X=[1,x], so

X′X=(n∑Xi∑Xi∑Xi2),X′Y=(∑Yi∑XiYi).\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X} = \begin{pmatrix} n & \sum X_i \\ \sum X_i & \sum X_i^2 \end{pmatrix}, \qquad \mathbf{X}'\mathbf{Y} = \begin{pmatrix} \sum Y_i \\ \sum X_i Y_i \end{pmatrix} .X′X=(n∑Xi​​∑Xi​∑Xi2​​),X′Y=(∑Yi​∑Xi​Yi​​).

where Sxx=∑(Xi−Xˉ)2S_{xx} = \sum (X_i - \bar{X})^2Sxx​=∑(Xi​−Xˉ)2 and Sxy=∑(Xi−Xˉ)(Yi−Yˉ)S_{xy} = \sum (X_i - \bar{X})(Y_i - \bar{Y})Sxy​=∑(Xi​−Xˉ)(Yi​−Yˉ) are the Chapter 2 sums of squares and cross-products, and Xˉ,Yˉ\bar{X}, \bar{Y}Xˉ,Yˉ are the sample means. The inverse of a 2×22 \times 22×2 matrix divides by its determinant det⁡(X′X)=n∑Xi2−(∑Xi)2=n Sxx\det(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X}) = n\sum X_i^2 - (\sum X_i)^2 = n\,S_{xx}det(X′X)=n∑Xi2​−(∑Xi​)2=nSxx​ (6.4 The inverse and the normal equations),

(X′X)−1=1n Sxx(∑Xi2−∑Xi−∑Xin).(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1} = \frac{1}{n\,S_{xx}}\begin{pmatrix} \sum X_i^2 & -\sum X_i \\ -\sum X_i & n \end{pmatrix} .(X′X)−1=nSxx​1​(∑Xi2​−∑Xi​​−∑Xi​n​).

Multiplying by X′Y\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{Y}X′Y and simplifying (each entry collapses using ∑Xi=nXˉ\sum X_i = n\bar{X}∑Xi​=nXˉ and the definitions of Sxx,SxyS_{xx}, S_{xy}Sxx​,Sxy​) gives

b=(X′X)−1X′Y=(Yˉ−b1XˉSxy/Sxx),\mathbf{b} = (\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{Y} = \begin{pmatrix} \bar{Y} - b_1\bar{X} \\ S_{xy}/S_{xx} \end{pmatrix} ,b=(X′X)−1X′Y=(Yˉ−b1​XˉSxy​/Sxx​​),

which are exactly the Chapter 2 estimators b0=Yˉ−b1Xˉb_0 = \bar{Y} - b_1\bar{X}b0​=Yˉ−b1​Xˉ and b1=Sxy/Sxxb_1 = S_{xy}/S_{xx}b1​=Sxy​/Sxx​. The covariance matrix reduces the same way: the bottom-right entry of σ2(X′X)−1\sigma^2(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}σ2(X′X)−1 is σ2n/(nSxx)=σ2/Sxx\sigma^2 n / (n S_{xx}) = \sigma^2/S_{xx}σ2n/(nSxx​)=σ2/Sxx​, matching Var⁡{b1}\operatorname{Var}\{b_1\}Var{b1​}, and the top-left entry is σ2∑Xi2/(nSxx)=σ2(1/n+Xˉ2/Sxx)\sigma^2 \sum X_i^2/(n S_{xx}) = \sigma^2(1/n + \bar{X}^2/S_{xx})σ2∑Xi2​/(nSxx​)=σ2(1/n+Xˉ2/Sxx​), matching Var⁡{b0}\operatorname{Var}\{b_0\}Var{b0​} from 2.5 Sampling behavior and the Gauss-Markov theorem. ■\blacksquare■

Example 7.5 already confirmed this numerically: the matrix pipeline returned Toluca’s b0=62.3659b_0 = 62.3659b0​=62.3659, b1=3.5702b_1 = 3.5702b1​=3.5702, and standard errors 26.1774 and 0.3470, the same figures Chapter 2 produced from the scalar formulas. The general linear model does not replace simple regression; it contains it.

7.8 Chapter summary¶

This chapter turned Chapter 6’s matrix arithmetic into a full multiple regression. One equation, Y=Xβ+ε\mathbf{Y} = \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta} + \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}Y=Xβ+ε, carries any number of predictors. Least squares projects Y\mathbf{Y}Y onto the column space of X\mathbf{X}X, giving b=(X′X)−1X′Y\mathbf{b} = (\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{Y}b=(X′X)−1X′Y by calculus and by geometry alike. The hat matrix H\mathbf{H}H packages that projection, and its two properties, being a projection (symmetric and idempotent) with trace ppp, drive the sampling distribution of b\mathbf{b}b, the n−pn - pn−p divisor in MSE\mathrm{MSE}MSE, and the Gauss-Markov optimality. Every Chapter 2 formula returns as the p=2p = 2p=2 special case.

Key results at a glance.

ResultStatement or formulaValid when
Least-squares estimator (Theorem 7.3)b=(X′X)−1X′Y\mathbf{b} = (\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{Y}b=(X′X)−1X′Y minimizes Q(b)Q(\mathbf{b})Q(b)X\mathbf{X}X full column rank
Normal equations (Definition 7.4)X′Xb=X′Y\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X}\mathbf{b} = \mathbf{X}'\mathbf{Y}X′Xb=X′Y, equivalently X′e=0\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{e} = \mathbf{0}X′e=0any least-squares fit
Hat matrix properties (Theorem 7.7)H\mathbf{H}H symmetric, idempotent, tr⁡H=p\operatorname{tr}\mathbf{H} = ptrH=p, tr⁡(I−H)=n−p\operatorname{tr}(\mathbf{I}-\mathbf{H}) = n-ptr(I−H)=n−pX\mathbf{X}X full column rank
Mean and covariance of b\mathbf{b}b (Theorem 7.8)E{b}=βE\{\mathbf{b}\} = \boldsymbol{\beta}E{b}=β, Cov⁡{b}=σ2(X′X)−1\operatorname{Cov}\{\mathbf{b}\} = \sigma^2(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}Cov{b}=σ2(X′X)−1E{ε}=0E\{\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}\}=\mathbf{0}E{ε}=0, Cov⁡{ε}=σ2I\operatorname{Cov}\{\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}\}=\sigma^2\mathbf{I}Cov{ε}=σ2I
Sampling distribution (Theorem 7.8)b∼N(β,σ2(X′X)−1)\mathbf{b} \sim N(\boldsymbol{\beta}, \sigma^2(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1})b∼N(β,σ2(X′X)−1)errors also normal
Unbiasedness of MSE (Theorem 7.9)E{SSE}=(n−p)σ2E\{\mathrm{SSE}\} = (n-p)\sigma^2E{SSE}=(n−p)σ2, so E{MSE}=σ2E\{\mathrm{MSE}\} = \sigma^2E{MSE}=σ2Cov⁡{ε}=σ2I\operatorname{Cov}\{\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}\}=\sigma^2\mathbf{I}Cov{ε}=σ2I
Gauss-Markov (Theorem 7.10)b\mathbf{b}b is the best linear unbiased estimator of β\boldsymbol{\beta}βE{ε}=0E\{\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}\}=\mathbf{0}E{ε}=0, Cov⁡{ε}=σ2I\operatorname{Cov}\{\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}\}=\sigma^2\mathbf{I}Cov{ε}=σ2I; no normality needed
Coefficient of determinationR2=1−SSE/SSTOR^2 = 1 - \mathrm{SSE}/\mathrm{SSTO}R2=1−SSE/SSTO (Dwaine 0.9167)descriptive, any fit

Key terms. general linear model, design matrix, normal equations, least-squares estimator, column space, hat matrix, idempotent matrix, full column rank, error degrees of freedom, best linear unbiased estimator (BLUE).

You should now be able to.

  • Write any linear regression as Y=Xβ+ε\mathbf{Y} = \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta} + \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}Y=Xβ+ε and identify the design matrix, coefficient vector, and error vector.

  • Derive b=(X′X)−1X′Y\mathbf{b} = (\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{Y}b=(X′X)−1X′Y two ways: by calculus and by orthogonal projection.

  • Interpret least squares geometrically as projecting Y\mathbf{Y}Y onto the column space of X\mathbf{X}X, with the residual perpendicular to it.

  • Prove that the hat matrix H\mathbf{H}H is symmetric and idempotent with trace ppp, and use it to write fitted values and residuals.

  • Derive the mean and covariance of b\mathbf{b}b and state its sampling distribution under normal errors.

  • Prove that s2=MSE=SSE/(n−p)s^2 = \mathrm{MSE} = \mathrm{SSE}/(n-p)s2=MSE=SSE/(n−p) is unbiased for σ2\sigma^2σ2 using the trace of I−H\mathbf{I}-\mathbf{H}I−H.

  • State and prove the Gauss-Markov theorem in matrix form: b\mathbf{b}b is the best linear unbiased estimator.

  • Recover every simple-regression formula from Chapter 2 as the special case p=2p = 2p=2.

Where this fits. This chapter is the general engine for the FIT stage of the course workflow (The modeling workflow): it estimates the coefficients and the error variance for a model of any size, replacing the predictor-by-predictor algebra of Chapters 2 and 3 with one formula that a computer can run for ten predictors as easily as one. It also arms the CHECK stage, because the hat matrix H\mathbf{H}H is the object Chapter 9 (9. Model diagnostics) turns into the leverage values and influence measures, and the USE stage, because Cov⁡{b}\operatorname{Cov}\{\mathbf{b}\}Cov{b} is what Chapter 8 turns into tests and intervals. Chapter 8 takes the Dwaine fit built here, interprets each coefficient with care, and develops the extra-sums-of-squares and general-linear-test machinery (8.3 Extra sums of squares) that decides which predictors a model needs.

7.9 Frequently asked questions¶

Q1. Why does the design matrix need a column of ones? The column of ones lets the intercept β0\beta_0β0​ be handled like any other coefficient: its “predictor value” is 1 for every observation, so β0⋅1\beta_0 \cdot 1β0​⋅1 is the same baseline for every row. Without it the fitted plane would be forced through the origin. The first normal equation, coming from that column, is exactly ∑ei=0\sum e_i = 0∑ei​=0.

Q2. What does full column rank mean, and what breaks without it? Full column rank means no predictor column is a linear combination of the others (including the ones column). If one is, say a predictor recorded in both meters and feet, then X′X\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X}X′X is singular and (X′X)−1(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}(X′X)−1 does not exist, so there is no unique b\mathbf{b}b: infinitely many coefficient vectors fit equally well. Software then drops a column or returns an error. Near-violations (highly correlated predictors) are multicollinearity, the subject of Chapter 12.

Q3. Is the hat matrix worth computing for real data? As a full n×nn \times nn×n matrix, usually not; software gets fitted values and residuals more cheaply. But its diagonal entries hiih_{ii}hii​, the leverage values, are computed and used constantly in diagnostics, and its algebra (symmetric, idempotent, trace ppp) is what makes the theory work. Think of H\mathbf{H}H as a proof tool and a source of the leverages, not a thing you print.

Q4. Why is the divisor n−pn - pn−p and not n−1n - 1n−1 or nnn? Each parameter you estimate uses one degree of freedom before the residuals are measured. A sample variance estimates one quantity (the mean) and divides by n−1n - 1n−1; a regression with ppp parameters divides by n−pn - pn−p. The trace derivation in 7.5 Estimating the error variance shows E{SSE}=(n−p)σ2E\{\mathrm{SSE}\} = (n - p)\sigma^2E{SSE}=(n−p)σ2 exactly, so n−pn - pn−p is the unique divisor making MSE\mathrm{MSE}MSE unbiased. Dividing by nnn (the maximum-likelihood choice) is biased low.

Q5. Do I need normal errors for any of this? No, for most of it. The estimator b\mathbf{b}b, its unbiasedness, its covariance σ2(X′X)−1\sigma^2(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}σ2(X′X)−1, the unbiasedness of MSE\mathrm{MSE}MSE, and the Gauss-Markov optimality all hold with only mean-zero, constant-variance, uncorrelated errors. Normality is an extra assumption, used to get the exact multivariate normal sampling distribution of b\mathbf{b}b and the ttt and FFF tests of Chapter 8.

Q6. The Dwaine coefficients from the matrix formula and from lm matched exactly. Is that always guaranteed? Yes, up to tiny floating-point differences. Both compute the same (X′X)−1X′Y(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{Y}(X′X)−1X′Y; lm and statsmodels use a more numerically stable routine (a QR or SVD decomposition) instead of forming the inverse explicitly, which matters only for badly conditioned designs. For well-behaved data like these, every printed digit agrees.

Q7. Why are the two Dwaine slope estimates negatively correlated in Figure 8? Because the two predictors are themselves positively correlated across cities. When a sample happens to make the population slope come out high, the fit compensates by making the income slope come out low to avoid double-counting the shared signal. That trade-off is exactly the off-diagonal entry of σ2(X′X)−1\sigma^2(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}σ2(X′X)−1, and it is why you cannot read one coefficient’s uncertainty without the whole covariance matrix.

7.10 Practice problems¶

Note

Unless a problem says otherwise, use dwaine.csv (with n=21n = 21n=21, p=3p = 3p=3, fitted coefficients (−68.86,1.4546,9.3655)(-68.86, 1.4546, 9.3655)(−68.86,1.4546,9.3655), MSE=121.16\mathrm{MSE} = 121.16MSE=121.16, R2=0.9167R^2 = 0.9167R2=0.9167) or toluca.csv (with n=25n = 25n=25, p=2p = 2p=2). Problems are marked (A) concepts, (B) theory, or (C) data analysis. Odd-numbered answers appear in Appendix H; full solutions are in the instructor materials.

  1. (A) Write the Dwaine model in the form Y=Xβ+ε\mathbf{Y} = \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta} + \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}Y=Xβ+ε, giving the dimensions of each of the four objects and describing the first column of X\mathbf{X}X.

  2. (A) Explain in one sentence each what X′X\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X}X′X and X′Y\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{Y}X′Y represent, and why the normal equations use them.

  3. (A) State the two error assumptions E{ε}=0E\{\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}\} = \mathbf{0}E{ε}=0 and Cov⁡{ε}=σ2I\operatorname{Cov}\{\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}\} = \sigma^2\mathbf{I}Cov{ε}=σ2I in plain words, saying what the diagonal and the off-diagonal of σ2I\sigma^2\mathbf{I}σ2I each encode.

  4. (A) In the projection picture, what is the column space of X\mathbf{X}X, where does Y^\hat{\mathbf{Y}}Y^ sit, and in what direction does the residual point? Answer without formulas.

  5. (A) Give the three properties of the hat matrix proved in this chapter and say what each one means geometrically.

  6. (A) Why is the divisor in MSE\mathrm{MSE}MSE equal to n−pn - pn−p? Answer by naming what the number ppp counts and what n−pn - pn−p counts.

  7. (A) A colleague says “with normality the least-squares estimator is BLUE; without it, it is not.” Correct the statement, naming exactly which assumptions the Gauss-Markov theorem uses.

  8. (A) Explain why XX′\mathbf{X}\mathbf{X}'XX′ (an n×nn \times nn×n matrix) cannot replace X′X\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X}X′X in the normal equations.

  9. (B) Starting from Q(b)=(Y−Xb)′(Y−Xb)Q(\mathbf{b}) = (\mathbf{Y} - \mathbf{X}\mathbf{b})'(\mathbf{Y} - \mathbf{X}\mathbf{b})Q(b)=(Y−Xb)′(Y−Xb), expand it, take the gradient, and derive the normal equations and b=(X′X)−1X′Y\mathbf{b} = (\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{Y}b=(X′X)−1X′Y (Theorem 7.3). Justify why the stationary point is a minimum.

  10. (B) Prove the projection identity ∥Y−Xb∗∥2=∥e∥2+∥X(b−b∗)∥2\|\mathbf{Y} - \mathbf{X}\mathbf{b}^{\ast}\|^2 = \|\mathbf{e}\|^2 + \|\mathbf{X}(\mathbf{b} - \mathbf{b}^{\ast})\|^2∥Y−Xb∗∥2=∥e∥2+∥X(b−b∗)∥2 for any b∗\mathbf{b}^{\ast}b∗, showing the cross term vanishes, and conclude that b\mathbf{b}b uniquely minimizes SSE.

  11. (B) Prove that H=X(X′X)−1X′\mathbf{H} = \mathbf{X}(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}'H=X(X′X)−1X′ is symmetric and idempotent (Theorem 7.7). Then prove I−H\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{H}I−H is symmetric and idempotent as well.

  12. (B) Prove tr⁡(H)=p\operatorname{tr}(\mathbf{H}) = ptr(H)=p using the cyclic property of the trace, and deduce tr⁡(I−H)=n−p\operatorname{tr}(\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{H}) = n - ptr(I−H)=n−p.

  13. (B) Show HX=X\mathbf{H}\mathbf{X} = \mathbf{X}HX=X and use it to prove (I−H)X=0(\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{H})\mathbf{X} = \mathbf{0}(I−H)X=0. Explain in words why the hat matrix leaves the columns of X\mathbf{X}X unchanged.

  14. (B) Derive E{b}=βE\{\mathbf{b}\} = \boldsymbol{\beta}E{b}=β and Cov⁡{b}=σ2(X′X)−1\operatorname{Cov}\{\mathbf{b}\} = \sigma^2(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}Cov{b}=σ2(X′X)−1 (Theorem 7.8), naming the random-vector facts you use at each step. Then, adding the assumption ε∼N(0,σ2I)\boldsymbol{\varepsilon} \sim N(\mathbf{0}, \sigma^2\mathbf{I})ε∼N(0,σ2I), state the full sampling distribution of b\mathbf{b}b and explain why the mean and covariance did not need normality but this distributional statement does.

  15. (B) Prove the quadratic-form lemma E{Y′AY}=tr⁡(AΣ)+μ′AμE\{\mathbf{Y}'\mathbf{A}\mathbf{Y}\} = \operatorname{tr}(\mathbf{A}\boldsymbol{\Sigma}) + \boldsymbol{\mu}'\mathbf{A}\boldsymbol{\mu}E{Y′AY}=tr(AΣ)+μ′Aμ, then use it to show E{SSE}=(n−p)σ2E\{\mathrm{SSE}\} = (n - p)\sigma^2E{SSE}=(n−p)σ2 (Theorem 7.9).

  16. (B) State and prove the Gauss-Markov theorem in matrix form (Theorem 7.10). Where in the proof does the assumption Cov⁡{ε}=σ2I\operatorname{Cov}\{\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}\} = \sigma^2\mathbf{I}Cov{ε}=σ2I enter, and where does full column rank enter?

  17. (B) For the simple-regression design X=[1,x]\mathbf{X} = [\mathbf{1}, \mathbf{x}]X=[1,x], compute X′X\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X}X′X, its determinant, and (X′X)−1(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}(X′X)−1, and derive b1=Sxy/Sxxb_1 = S_{xy}/S_{xx}b1​=Sxy​/Sxx​ and b0=Yˉ−b1Xˉb_0 = \bar{Y} - b_1\bar{X}b0​=Yˉ−b1​Xˉ from the matrix formula.

  18. (B) Using the reduction in problem 17, derive Var⁡{b1}=σ2/Sxx\operatorname{Var}\{b_1\} = \sigma^2/S_{xx}Var{b1​}=σ2/Sxx​ and Var⁡{b0}=σ2(1/n+Xˉ2/Sxx)\operatorname{Var}\{b_0\} = \sigma^2(1/n + \bar{X}^2/S_{xx})Var{b0​}=σ2(1/n+Xˉ2/Sxx​) from σ2(X′X)−1\sigma^2(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}σ2(X′X)−1.

  19. (B) Show that Cov⁡{e,Y^}=0\operatorname{Cov}\{\mathbf{e}, \hat{\mathbf{Y}}\} = \mathbf{0}Cov{e,Y^}=0 under the model, using Y^=HY\hat{\mathbf{Y}} = \mathbf{H}\mathbf{Y}Y^=HY, e=(I−H)Y\mathbf{e} = (\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{H})\mathbf{Y}e=(I−H)Y, and H(I−H)=0\mathbf{H}(\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{H}) = \mathbf{0}H(I−H)=0. Why does this matter for diagnostics?

  20. (B) Prove that adding a predictor (a new column to X\mathbf{X}X) cannot increase SSE\mathrm{SSE}SSE, using the projection argument that enlarging the column space can only move Y^\hat{\mathbf{Y}}Y^ closer to Y\mathbf{Y}Y.

  21. (C) Read dwaine.csv, build X\mathbf{X}X and Y\mathbf{Y}Y, and compute b\mathbf{b}b from (X′X)−1X′Y(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{Y}(X′X)−1X′Y in R or Python. Confirm your answer matches lm / smf.ols.

  22. (C) For the Dwaine fit, compute the residual vector and verify numerically that X′e=0\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{e} = \mathbf{0}X′e=0 (all three entries) and that ∑Y^i=∑Yi\sum \hat{Y}_i = \sum Y_i∑Y^i​=∑Yi​.

  23. (C) Build the Dwaine hat matrix and verify it is symmetric and idempotent and has trace 3. Then report the five largest diagonal entries hiih_{ii}hii​ and say what a large hiih_{ii}hii​ indicates about that city’s predictors.

  24. (C) Compute SSE\mathrm{SSE}SSE, MSE\mathrm{MSE}MSE, and sss for Dwaine two ways: from the residual vector, and from summary / .summary2(). Then compute SSE/n\mathrm{SSE}/nSSE/n and report the percentage by which it understates MSE\mathrm{MSE}MSE.

  25. (C) Compute the estimated covariance matrix MSE (X′X)−1\mathrm{MSE}\,(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}MSE(X′X)−1 for Dwaine, report the three standard errors, and confirm they match the built-in summary. Report the correlation between the two slope estimates from the off-diagonal entry.

  26. (C) Refit Toluca through the matrix formulas and confirm b0=62.3659b_0 = 62.3659b0​=62.3659, b1=3.5702b_1 = 3.5702b1​=3.5702, MSE=2383.72\mathrm{MSE} = 2383.72MSE=2383.72, and the two standard errors, matching Chapter 2 to every digit.

  27. (C) Write a simulation (seed 4210) that draws 2000 datasets from the fitted Dwaine model, refits each, and reports the mean of the 2000 estimates of each coefficient (checking unbiasedness) and the standard deviation of the target-population slope (checking Cov⁡{b}\operatorname{Cov}\{\mathbf{b}\}Cov{b}).

  28. (C) Regress Dwaine sales on targtpop alone, then on targtpop and dispoinc together, and confirm numerically that adding dispoinc lowers SSE\mathrm{SSE}SSE. Relate the drop to the projection argument in problem 20.

7.11 Exam practice¶

Note

These five problems are written in the style of the course exams: each one asks you to explain in full sentences, not just to produce a number. Work each answer before opening its model answer. The model answers show the depth that earns full marks, and each ends with a note on what a weak answer leaves out. Datasets are dwaine.csv (n=21n = 21n=21, p=3p = 3p=3) and toluca.csv (n=25n = 25n=25, p=2p = 2p=2), the same fits used throughout the chapter.

EP 7.1 (interpret output in context). A colleague fits the Dwaine model in R and reads off the coefficient table.

            Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)
(Intercept) -68.8571    60.0170 -1.1473   0.2663
targtpop      1.4546     0.2118  6.8682   0.0000
dispoinc      9.3655     4.0640  2.3045   0.0333

Interpret the targtpop coefficient and its ttt value in the context of studio sales, being explicit about what is held fixed. Then evaluate the colleague’s conclusion: “the intercept has p=0.27p = 0.27p=0.27, so it is not significantly different from zero, which means the model is misspecified and we should drop the intercept.” Say whether the conclusion is right and why.

Model answer

Holding disposable income fixed, each additional thousand young residents in a city is associated with about 1.4546 thousand dollars, roughly $1{,}455, of additional predicted annual sales. The ttt value of 6.87 is the estimate divided by its standard error (1.4546/0.21181.4546 / 0.21181.4546/0.2118); it is far from zero and its ppp-value is essentially zero, so the target-population slope is clearly distinguishable from zero at any usual level, and target population carries real predictive signal once income is already in the model.

The colleague’s conclusion is wrong on both counts. The intercept’s insignificance means only that the data cannot pin down the fitted plane’s height at the point where both predictors equal zero, which is far outside the range of any real city (populations near 62 thousand and incomes near 17 thousand dollars in these data), so a loose intercept there is expected and harmless. An insignificant intercept is not evidence of misspecification, and dropping the intercept is a strong, usually wrong, modeling choice: it forces the fitted plane through the origin, distorts every slope, and breaks the identity ∑ei=0\sum e_i = 0∑ei​=0 that the column of ones guarantees. Keep the intercept.

A weak answer reports the slope as a raw number without the “holding income fixed” comparison or the units, and treats the intercept’s large ppp-value as a defect to fix rather than a statement about an extrapolated region no city occupies.

EP 7.2 (explain why, and what would change if). Explain in full sentences why the divisor in MSE=SSE/(n−p)\mathrm{MSE} = \mathrm{SSE}/(n - p)MSE=SSE/(n−p) is n−pn - pn−p and not nnn. Then answer a “what would change if”: suppose an analyst kept adding predictors to the Dwaine data until the model had p=21p = 21p=21 parameters, one for every observation. Describe what happens to SSE\mathrm{SSE}SSE, to the divisor, and to MSE\mathrm{MSE}MSE, and explain why a fit with SSE=0\mathrm{SSE} = 0SSE=0 is a warning rather than a success.

Model answer

The divisor is n−pn - pn−p because fitting ppp coefficients uses up ppp of the nnn independent directions in the data before the residuals are measured. Formally, SSE=Y′(I−H)Y\mathrm{SSE} = \mathbf{Y}'(\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{H})\mathbf{Y}SSE=Y′(I−H)Y and tr⁡(I−H)=n−p\operatorname{tr}(\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{H}) = n - ptr(I−H)=n−p, so E{SSE}=(n−p)σ2E\{\mathrm{SSE}\} = (n - p)\sigma^2E{SSE}=(n−p)σ2; dividing by n−pn - pn−p, and no other number, makes MSE\mathrm{MSE}MSE an unbiased estimator of σ2\sigma^2σ2. Dividing by nnn would systematically underestimate the error variance.

If p=21=np = 21 = np=21=n, the column space of X\mathbf{X}X fills the whole 21-dimensional response space, so the projection Y^=HY\hat{\mathbf{Y}} = \mathbf{H}\mathbf{Y}Y^=HY equals Y\mathbf{Y}Y exactly and every residual is zero, giving SSE=0\mathrm{SSE} = 0SSE=0. The divisor n−p=21−21=0n - p = 21 - 21 = 0n−p=21−21=0, so MSE=0/0\mathrm{MSE} = 0/0MSE=0/0 is undefined: there are no error degrees of freedom left. A zero SSE\mathrm{SSE}SSE does not mean the model is perfect; it means the model has enough parameters to interpolate the data exactly, so it has learned the noise along with the signal and can say nothing about σ2\sigma^2σ2 or about a new city. Perfect in-sample fit with zero degrees of freedom is the signature of overfitting.

A weak answer says “n−pn - pn−p adjusts for the parameters” without connecting it to unbiasedness or the trace, and treats SSE=0\mathrm{SSE} = 0SSE=0 as a good outcome instead of recognizing that zero error degrees of freedom leave nothing to estimate the variance with.

EP 7.3 (a student claims X, evaluate). A student writes: “Adding a predictor can never increase SSE\mathrm{SSE}SSE, and it always raises R2R^2R2. So the best strategy is to keep adding predictors until R2R^2R2 is as close to 1 as possible.” Using the projection picture of least squares, evaluate the two factual claims and the strategy they support.

Model answer

The two factual claims are true. Adding a column to X\mathbf{X}X enlarges its column space, and projecting Y\mathbf{Y}Y onto a larger subspace can only bring the foot of the perpendicular Y^\hat{\mathbf{Y}}Y^ closer to Y\mathbf{Y}Y, never farther, so SSE=∥Y−Y^∥2\mathrm{SSE} = \|\mathbf{Y} - \hat{\mathbf{Y}}\|^2SSE=∥Y−Y^∥2 cannot rise; the old fit is still available as a special case where the new coefficient is zero. Because R2=1−SSE/SSTOR^2 = 1 - \mathrm{SSE}/\mathrm{SSTO}R2=1−SSE/SSTO and SSTO\mathrm{SSTO}SSTO is fixed, a non-increasing SSE\mathrm{SSE}SSE means a non-decreasing R2R^2R2.

The strategy, however, is wrong. Since R2R^2R2 rises even for predictors that are pure noise, a high R2R^2R2 bought by piling on predictors is not evidence of a good model; in the limit p=np = np=n the fit interpolates the data with R2=1R^2 = 1R2=1 and SSE=0\mathrm{SSE} = 0SSE=0 while explaining nothing out of sample. Each added predictor also spends a degree of freedom, shrinking n−pn - pn−p and inflating the variances σ2(X′X)−1\sigma^2(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}σ2(X′X)−1 of the coefficients, especially when the new predictor is correlated with those already present. The right question is not whether SSE\mathrm{SSE}SSE dropped but whether it dropped by more than noise would predict, which is exactly what the general linear test of Chapter 8 measures.

A weak answer either wrongly disputes the two true facts or accepts the strategy, missing that a guaranteed rise in R2R^2R2 carries no information about whether a predictor belongs in the model.

EP 7.4 (interpret computed output in context). The leverage values hiih_{ii}hii​ are the diagonal entries of the hat matrix H=X(X′X)−1X′\mathbf{H} = \mathbf{X}(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}'H=X(X′X)−1X′ for the Dwaine fit. The six largest, and a check on their sum, are below.

row 20  targtpop=  82.7  dispoinc= 19.1  h_ii=0.2788
row 13  targtpop=  88.4  dispoinc= 17.4  h_ii=0.2390
row 15  targtpop=  52.5  dispoinc= 17.8  h_ii=0.2095
row  3  targtpop=  91.3  dispoinc= 18.2  h_ii=0.1737
row  5  targtpop=  46.9  dispoinc= 17.3  h_ii=0.1620
row 19  targtpop=  89.6  dispoinc= 18.1  h_ii=0.1611
sum(h_ii) = 3.0000     mean(targtpop)=62.02  mean(dispoinc)=17.14

Explain what a large hiih_{ii}hii​ says about a city, and interpret why row 20 has the largest leverage value even though its target population (82.7) is not the largest in the data. Then explain why the leverage values sum to exactly 3, and use the rule-of-thumb cutoff 2p/n=0.2862p/n = 0.2862p/n=0.286 to say whether any Dwaine city should be called a high-leverage point.

Model answer

A large hiih_{ii}hii​ means the city sits far from the center of the predictor cloud, so its predictor values are unusual and its own response pulls its fitted value strongly toward itself. Row 20 has the largest leverage value not because of any single coordinate but because of the combination: its disposable income 19.1 is the highest in the data, well above the mean of 17.14, and its target population 82.7 is also well above the mean of 62.02, so it sits in a far corner of the two-dimensional predictor space. The leverage value measures distance from the joint center, so a city can have a higher leverage value than one with a larger population alone if it is more extreme on both predictors together.

The leverage values sum to 3 because ∑ihii=tr⁡(H)=p=3\sum_i h_{ii} = \operatorname{tr}(\mathbf{H}) = p = 3∑i​hii​=tr(H)=p=3, proved in 7.3 The hat matrix: the trace of the projection matrix counts the parameters, so the total of the leverage values the fit distributes across cities is fixed at ppp regardless of the data. Against the cutoff 2p/n=0.2862p/n = 0.2862p/n=0.286, the largest leverage value (0.2788) falls just below it, so by this rule of thumb no Dwaine city is flagged as a high-leverage point; the design is fairly balanced, with no city dominating the predictor space.
