A weak answer reads the leverage value off target population alone, misses that it measures joint distance from the center of both predictors, and cannot explain the sum-to-ppp property as the trace of H\mathbf{H}H.

EP 7.5 (what would change if). An analyst re-expresses target population in hundreds of people instead of thousands, multiplying every targtpop value by 10, and refits. The two coefficient tables are below.

original                              rescaled
             Estimate Std. Error       Estimate Std. Error
(Intercept)  -68.8571    60.0170       -68.8571    60.0170
targtpop       1.4546     0.2118        0.1455     0.0212
dispoinc       9.3655     4.0640        9.3655     4.0640
original  MSE=121.16  R2=0.9167     rescaled  MSE=121.16  R2=0.9167

Explain, in full sentences and with reference to the model algebra, why the target- population coefficient and its standard error both divide by 10 while its ttt value, the fitted values, MSE\mathrm{MSE}MSE, and R2R^2R2 are all unchanged. What does this tell you about reading the size of a coefficient as a measure of a predictor’s importance?

Model answer

Rescaling a predictor by a factor of 10 replaces its column x\mathbf{x}x by 10x10\mathbf{x}10x. For the fitted plane to make the same predictions, the coefficient on that column must divide by 10, since (10x)(β/10)=xβ(10\mathbf{x})(\beta/10) = \mathbf{x}\beta(10x)(β/10)=xβ: the product that enters Y^\hat{\mathbf{Y}}Y^ is unchanged, so the fitted values, the residuals, SSE\mathrm{SSE}SSE, MSE\mathrm{MSE}MSE, and R2R^2R2 are all identical to the original fit. The standard error divides by 10 for the same reason the estimate does, because it is measured in the coefficient’s own units (dollars per hundred people instead of dollars per thousand people), so the ratio t=b/s{b}t = b / s\{b\}t=b/s{b} is dimensionless and does not move; the evidence that the slope differs from zero is exactly as strong as before.

The lesson is that the raw magnitude of a coefficient is not a measure of a predictor’s importance, because you can make a coefficient ten times larger or smaller just by changing the units of its predictor, with no change to the fit or to the strength of the evidence. To compare importance you must look at scale-free quantities such as the ttt value, or standardize the predictors first.

A weak answer states that the numbers change without tying the factor of 10 to the xβ\mathbf{x}\betaxβ product staying fixed, and fails to draw the conclusion that coefficient size, being unit-dependent, cannot rank predictors by importance.

7.12 Chapter game¶

Play the Chapter 7 game

Play the Chapter 7 game on your phone or laptop: 10 quick rounds, no setup. Open the game

It drills the general linear model on the real Dwaine and Toluca data: reading a coefficient with the other predictor held fixed, ordering the (X′X)−1X′Y(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{Y}(X′X)−1X′Y pipeline, fitting least squares as a projection, spotting the high-leverage city, matching the hat-matrix objects, and using the n−pn - pn−p divisor. You get the reason after every answer.

Resumen del capítulo (en español)

Este capítulo presenta el modelo lineal general (general linear model) Y=Xβ+ε\mathbf{Y} = \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta} + \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}Y=Xβ+ε, que escribe cualquier regresión, con uno o con muchos predictores, en una sola forma matricial. La matriz de diseño (design matrix) X\mathbf{X}X tiene una primera columna de unos para el intercepto y una columna por cada predictor; β\boldsymbol{\beta}β reúne los parámetros y ε\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}ε los errores, con E{ε}=0E\{\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}\} = \mathbf{0}E{ε}=0 y Cov⁡{ε}=σ2I\operatorname{Cov}\{\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}\} = \sigma^2\mathbf{I}Cov{ε}=σ2I.

El estimador de mínimos cuadrados (least squares) es b=(X′X)−1X′Y\mathbf{b} = (\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{Y}b=(X′X)−1X′Y, obtenido de dos maneras: por cálculo, derivando las ecuaciones normales (normal equations) X′Xb=X′Y\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X}\mathbf{b} = \mathbf{X}'\mathbf{Y}X′Xb=X′Y; y por proyección ortogonal (orthogonal projection), donde Y^\hat{\mathbf{Y}}Y^ es el punto del espacio de columnas de X\mathbf{X}X más cercano a Y\mathbf{Y}Y y el residuo e=Y−Y^\mathbf{e} = \mathbf{Y} - \hat{\mathbf{Y}}e=Y−Y^ es perpendicular a ese espacio. Para Dwaine Studios, b=(−68.86, 1.4546, 9.3655)\mathbf{b} = (-68.86,\ 1.4546,\ 9.3655)b=(−68.86, 1.4546, 9.3655).

La matriz sombrero (hat matrix) H=X(X′X)−1X′\mathbf{H} = \mathbf{X}(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}'H=X(X′X)−1X′ cumple Y^=HY\hat{\mathbf{Y}} = \mathbf{H}\mathbf{Y}Y^=HY y es simétrica e idempotente con traza ppp. De ahí sale que tr⁡(I−H)=n−p\operatorname{tr}(\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{H}) = n - ptr(I−H)=n−p, el divisor correcto para estimar la varianza: MSE=SSE/(n−p)\mathrm{MSE} = \mathrm{SSE}/(n - p)MSE=SSE/(n−p) es insesgado (unbiased), lo cual se prueba con un argumento de traza. Para Dwaine, MSE=121.16\mathrm{MSE} = 121.16MSE=121.16 y R2=0.9167R^2 = 0.9167R2=0.9167.

El vector b\mathbf{b}b tiene media β\boldsymbol{\beta}β y covarianza σ2(X′X)−1\sigma^2(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}σ2(X′X)−1; bajo errores normales sigue una distribución normal multivariante. El teorema de Gauss-Markov (Gauss-Markov theorem) demuestra que b\mathbf{b}b es el mejor estimador lineal insesgado, sin necesitar normalidad. Finalmente, la regresión simple del Capítulo 2 aparece como el caso p=2p = 2p=2: las fórmulas matriciales reproducen b0=62.3659b_0 = 62.3659b0​=62.3659, b1=3.5702b_1 = 3.5702b1​=3.5702 y MSE=2383.72\mathrm{MSE} = 2383.72MSE=2383.72 de la Toluca Company, dígito por dígito.

Part II: The linear model in matrix form
6. Matrix algebra for regression
Part II: The linear model in matrix form
8. Multiple regression in practice
